specification.topic-statement: The terminal setup component initializes a TTY file descriptor into raw mode with an alternate screen buffer, probes for enhanced keyboard protocol support, and restores the terminal to its original state on cleanup.

scope.in-scope: Headless terminal creation, terminal initialization from a TTY file descriptor, CSI u protocol probing and activation, terminal size querying, and terminal cleanup and state restoration.
scope.boundary: Normal (non-headless) initialization opens /dev/tty directly. The test-facing variant accepts a pre-opened file descriptor. All terminal I/O — escape sequences, attribute changes, size queries — goes through this descriptor.
CSI u enable and unexpected-response events are written to the logger when one is provided. Logging is optional; no logger produces no log output.

field.name: screen_rows
field.note: terminal height in rows; 50 in headless mode

field.name: screen_cols
field.note: terminal width in columns; 100 in headless mode

field.name: csi_u_supported
field.note: whether enhanced keyboard protocol was activated; false in headless mode

field.name: orig_termios
field.note: saved original terminal settings for restoration on cleanup

behavior.headless-initialization: When headless initialization is requested, a terminal context is returned with a file descriptor of -1, 50 rows, 100 columns, and CSI u marked as not supported. No TTY is opened and no I/O is performed.
behavior.normal-initialization:
Terminal initialization takes a TTY file descriptor and proceeds through the following steps in order. If any step fails, preceding state changes are rolled back before the error is returned.
The current terminal attributes are saved. Failure to retrieve them closes the file descriptor and returns an I/O error.
Terminal attributes are modified from the saved copy: - Input flags cleared: BRKINT, ICRNL, INPCK, ISTRIP, IXON - Output flags cleared: OPOST - Control flags set: CS8 - Local flags cleared: ECHO, ICANON, IEXTEN, ISIG - Minimum read size set to 1; read timeout set to 0
The modified attributes are applied immediately (no drain). Failure closes the file descriptor and returns an I/O error.
Any bytes already queued in the input buffer from before raw mode was active are discarded. Failure restores the original terminal attributes, closes the file descriptor, and returns an I/O error.
The escape sequence to enter the alternate screen buffer (ESC[?1049h, 8 bytes) is written to the TTY. Failure restores original attributes, closes the file descriptor, and returns an I/O error.
The escape sequence to clear the entire screen and move the cursor to the home position (ESC[2J ESC[H, 6 bytes) is written to the TTY. Failure restores original attributes, closes the file descriptor, and returns an I/O error.
On screen-clear failure, the code restores terminal attributes and closes the file descriptor but does not send the alternate-screen exit sequence. The terminal is left in the alternate screen buffer.
The enhanced keyboard protocol (CSI u) is probed by sending the query sequence (ESC[?u, 4 bytes) and waiting up to 100 milliseconds for a response. If a response of at least 4 bytes arrives and begins with ESC [ ?, and a 'u' character appears anywhere after the third byte, the protocol is considered supported. Any other outcome — timeout, read error, response shorter than 4 bytes, missing ESC[? prefix, or no 'u' terminator — means the protocol is not supported.
CSI u support failure is non-fatal. Initialization continues regardless of probe outcome.
If CSI u support was detected, the enable sequence (ESC[>9u, 5 bytes, flag value 9 which requests key disambiguation and all-key reporting) is sent. The system waits up to 100 milliseconds for a response.
If no response arrives within the timeout, the protocol is assumed to have been successfully enabled and the terminal context records it as active.
If a response arrives and begins with ESC [ ?, and a 'u' character appears after the third byte, the decimal digits preceding 'u' are parsed as the enabled flags value. A log event named "csi_u_enabled" carrying the flags value is written to the logger. The protocol is recorded as active.
If a response arrives but does not match the expected ESC[?…u format, a log event named "csi_u_unexpected_response" is written, carrying the response length in bytes and a space-separated lowercase hexadecimal dump of up to the first 32 bytes of the response. Despite the unexpected format, the protocol is still recorded as active.
If the read call returns zero or a negative value, the enable attempt fails and CSI u is recorded as inactive.
Any response that does not match the expected format still causes CSI u to be recorded as active. Only a zero or error return from the read actually disables it.
The terminal size is queried from the system. Failure exits the alternate screen buffer (ESC[?1049l), restores original attributes, closes the file descriptor, and returns an I/O error. On success, the row and column counts are stored in the terminal context.
behavior.size-query: When the terminal size is queried after initialization, if the file descriptor is valid, the size is re-read from the system and the stored values are updated. If the file descriptor is -1 (headless), the stored values are returned without any system call. Failure to query a live terminal size returns an I/O error.
behavior.cleanup:
Terminal cleanup is a no-op if the context is absent or the file descriptor is -1. Otherwise, the following steps are performed in order:
1. If CSI u was active, the disable sequence (ESC[<u, 4 bytes) is written. 2. The alternate screen exit sequence (ESC[?1049l, 8 bytes) is written. 3. The original terminal attributes are restored immediately (no drain). 4. Any remaining queued input is discarded. 5. The file descriptor is closed.
All write and flush failures are silently ignored during cleanup. Freeing the terminal context is the caller's responsibility.
